critique.summary: The article introduces tinygrad, a simple neural network framework, and promotes the tinybox, a powerful deep learning computer with various configurations and high performance metrics. It highlights tinygrad's features, the tinybox's hardware specifications, pricing, and availability, as well as the company's hiring and development plans.
mainPoints: tinygrad is a minimalistic neural network framework that simplifies complex networks into three operation types.
tinybox is a deep learning computer available in different models (red, green, blackwell, exabox) with varying GPU power, RAM, and bandwidth.
The tinybox is claimed to offer high performance at a competitive price, benchmarked against more expensive systems in MLPerf Training 4.0.
tinygrad supports both inference and training with autodiff, and is used in applications like openpilot for driving models.
tinygrad development is ongoing on GitHub and Discord, with plans to improve speed and stability.
tinybox is currently available for purchase with fixed configurations and wire transfer payment only.
critique.truthfulness: The technical descriptions of tinygrad and tinybox align with publicly available information on the tinygrad GitHub repository and tinybox product pages, which confirm the framework's simplicity and the hardware specifications of the tinybox models. The claim about MLPerf Training 4.0 benchmarking is plausible but lacks direct citation or third-party verification in the article. The use of tinygrad in openpilot is consistent with known open-source projects. However, some performance claims, such as achieving 'likely the best performance/$' and the exabox's 'exaFLOP' scale, should be independently verified as they are promotional and not supported by external benchmarks in the text. Overall, the article is mostly accurate but promotional and lacks multiple independent sources.
considerations: The article does not provide independent third-party benchmarks or reviews to substantiate performance and cost-effectiveness claims.
No mention of potential limitations or comparisons with other popular frameworks and hardware beyond brief MLPerf reference.
The pricing and availability details may change and should be verified directly from the vendor.
The article lacks discussion on software ecosystem maturity, community support, and compatibility with common ML tools beyond tinygrad.
No information on warranty, customer support, or long-term maintenance for tinybox buyers.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the Tinybox deep learning hardware, with commenters debating its pricing, target market, and technical specifications. Many express skepticism about its value proposition compared to custom builds or existing alternatives, while some appreciate the concept and design.

comments.author: bastawhiz
comments.text:
There's no way the red v2 is doing anything with a 120b parameter model. I just finished building a dual a100 ai homelab (80gb vram combined with nvlink). Similar stats otherwise. 120b only fits with very heavy quantization, enough to make the model schizophrenic in my experience. And there's no room for kv, so you'll OOM around 4k of context.

I'm running a 70b model now that's okay, but it's still fairly tight. And I've got 16gb more vram then the red v2.

I'm also confused why this is 12U. My whole rig is 4u.

The green v2 has better GPUs. But for $65k, I'd expect a much better CPU and 256gb of RAM. It's not like a threadripper 7000 is going to break the bank.

I'm glad this exists but it's... honestly pretty perplexing
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed technical insight based on personal experience, questioning the feasibility of the claimed 120B parameter model support and hardware specs.

comments.author: roarcher
comments.text:
> In order to keep prices low and quality high, we don't offer any customization to the box or ordering process. If you aren't capable of ordering through the website, I'm sorry but we won't be able to help.

Has this guy never worked on a B2B product before? Nobody is going to order a $10 million piece of infrastructure through your website's order form. And they are definitely going to want to negotiate something, even if it's just a warranty. And you'll do it because they're waving a $10 million check in your face.

The tone of this website is arrogant to the point of being almost hostile. The guy behind this seems to think that his name carries enough weight to dictate terms like this, among other things like requiring candidates to have already contributed to his product to even be considered for a job. I would be extremely surprised if anyone except him thinks he's that important.
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about the sales approach and tone of the company, reflecting realistic B2B purchasing behaviors and skepticism about the founder's attitude.

comments.author: saidnooneever
comments.text:
its a bit weird to me ud need to be contributor to their software to work in operations or hardware, but I suppose its ok for tinycompany. in long term its likely better to have domain experts and not bias everything towards the same thing.

the boxes look cool but how good are they really? the cheapest box seems pricey at 12 for a what is essentially a few gaming gpus. i dont see why you couldnt make that like half the price. u could do a PC/server build thats much much faster for way less. size doesnt matter if its more than twice the price i think...

the more expensive box has atleast real processing gpus but afaik also not very popular ones, this one seems maybe more fair priced (there seems a big difference in bang for buck between these???).

the third one suggested looks like a joke.

dont get me wrong, this seems like a really cool idea. But i dont see it taking off as the prices are corporate but the product seems more home use.

maybe in time they will find a better balance, i do respect the fact that the component market now is sour as hell and making good products with stable prices is pretty much i possible.

id love one of these machines someday, maybe when i am less poor, or when they are xD.

(love the styling of everything, this is the most critical i could be from a dumb consumer perspective, which i totally am btw.)
comments.analysis: Offers a consumer perspective on pricing and product positioning, highlighting concerns about cost versus performance and company hiring policies.

comments.author: vessenes
comments.text:
The exabox is interesting. I wonder who the customer is; after watching the Vera Rubin launch, I cannot imagine deciding I wanted to compete with NVIDIA for hyperscale business right now. Maybe it’s aiming at a value-conscious buyer? Maybe it’s a sensible buy for a (relatively) cash-strapped ML startup; actually I just checked prices, and it looks like Vera Rubin costs half for a similar amount of GPU RAM. I’m certain that the interconnect will not be as good as NV’s.

I have no idea who would buy this. Maybe if you think Vera Rubin is three years out? But NV ships, man, they are shipping.
comments.analysis: Provides a market comparison and questions the target audience, showing awareness of competing products and pricing.

comments.author: alexfromapex
comments.text: $12,000 for the base model is insane. I have an Apple M3 Max with 128GB RAM that can run 120B parameter models using like 80 watts of electricity at about 15-20 tokens/sec.  It's not amazing for 120B parameter models but it's also not 12 grand.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about pricing by comparing to a known consumer device, though the comparison may oversimplify differences in use cases and performance.

comments.author: paxys
comments.text: The problem with all these "AI box" startups is that the product is too expensive for hobbyists, and companies that need to run workloads at scale can always build their own servers and racks and save on the markup (which is substantial). Unless someone can figure out how to get cheaper GPUs & RAM there is really no margin left to squeeze out.
comments.analysis: Highlights a common industry challenge regarding pricing and market segmentation, accurately noting the difficulty in competing with custom builds.

comments.author: alasdair_
comments.text:
I just don’t believe that this can run inference on a 120 billion parameter model at actually useful speeds.

Obviously any Turing machine can run any size of model, so the “120B” claim doesn’t mean much - what actually matters is speed and I just don’t believe this can be speedy enough on models that my $5000 5090-based pc is too slow for and lacks enough vram for.
comments.analysis: Skeptical about performance claims, emphasizing the importance of speed and VRAM capacity for practical inference.

comments.author: jmspring
comments.text:
Tinygrad devices are interesting, I wish I have screen captures - but their prices have gone up and some specs like RAM have gone down.

A single box with those specs without having to build/configure (the red and green) - I could see being useful if you had $ and not time to build/configure/etc yourself.
comments.analysis: Notes changes in pricing and specifications over time, suggesting possible concerns about value and product evolution.

comments.author: gymbeaux
comments.text:
$12,000 gets you 1Gb/s networking and vanilla Ubuntu 24.04. Napkin math on the hardware it looks like margins are around 50% which feels like a school fundraiser where everyone pays what is obviously way more than normal retail price for X because "it's for the children."

I'm not sure what tinygrad is but I assume the markup is because the customer is making a conscious choice to support the tinygrad project. But what's unusual is there is apparently no reason whatsoever to buy this hardware, even if you plan on using tinygrad exclusively for your project. At least with System76 hardware I get (in theory) first class support for Pop!_OS.
comments.analysis: Critiques the pricing and margin structure, implying the product is overpriced relative to hardware costs and lacks compelling reasons to buy.

comments.author: ivraatiems
comments.text:
There's some irony in the fact that this website reads as extremely NOT AI-generated, very human in the way it's designed and the tone of its writing.

Still, this is a great idea, and one I hope takes off. I think there's a good argument that the future of AI is in locally-trained models for everyone, rather than relying on a big company's own model.

One thought: The ability to conveniently get this onto a 240v circuit would be nice. Having to find two different 120v circuits to plug this into will be a pain for many folks.
comments.analysis: Offers a positive but nuanced view on the product's concept and presentation, including a practical suggestion about power supply compatibility.
